title: Divide fractions and whole numbers word problems
description: Solve and interpret division word problems that include whole numbers and unit fractions.
topic_path: Divide fractions and whole numbers word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
Billy has $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a kilogram of trail mix.  He wants to share it equally between himself and his brother.  

**How much trail mix would they each get?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilogram
hints:
Billy has $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of a kilogram of trail mix. He plans to split the trail mix among $\blueE{2}$ people.

So, $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\div\blueE2$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of a kilogram is $1$ kilogram divided into $4$ equal pieces.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$  piece into $\blueE2$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueE2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$.
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\div\purpleE2=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$
Each person will get $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ of a kilogram of trail mix.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jason has $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a liter of milk. Jason divides the milk equally into $4$ mugs of tea.

**How much milk will Jason have in each mug?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a liter
hints:
Jason has $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ of a liter of milk. He plans to divide the milk into $\blueE{4}$ mugs.

So, $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{3}}\div\blueE4$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ of a liter is $1$ liter divided into $3$ equal pieces.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{3}}$  piece into $\blueE4$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueE4$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$.
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{3}}\div\purpleE4=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$
Jason will have $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$ of a liter of milk in each of his mugs.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mary used $\dfrac{1}{7}$ of a spool of ribbon to complete $4$ projects.  She used the same amount of ribbon on each project.


**How much ribbon did Mary use on each project?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a spool
hints:
Mary has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{7}}$ of a spool of ribbon. She used the ribbon equally across $\blueD{4}$ projects.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{7}}\div\blueD4$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{7}}$ of a spool is $1$ spool divided into $7$ equal pieces.  
 
[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{7}}$  piece into $\blueD4$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD4$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{28}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{7}}\div\greenD4=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{28}}$
Mary used $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{28}}$ of a spool of ribbon on each project.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lisa has $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an hour to read and watch television.  She wants to split her time equally between the $2$ activities.  

**How much time would she spend on each activity?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of an hour
hints:
Lisa has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ of an hour. She wants to split this time across $\blueD{2}$ activities.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}\div\blueD2$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ of an hour is $1$ hour divided into $3$ equal pieces.  
 
[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$  piece into $\blueD2$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD2$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{3}}\div\greenD2=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$
Lisa will spend $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$ of an hour on each activity.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joe has $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a bag of peanuts.  He wants to share them equally among himself and $3$  friends.  

**How much of the bag of peanuts would each person get?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of the bag
hints:
Joe has $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ of a bag of peanuts. He wants to split the peanuts between $\blueE{4}$ people.

So, $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{2}}\div\blueE4$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ of a bag is $1$ bag divided into $2$ equal pieces.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{2}}$  piece into $\blueE4$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueE4$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$.
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{2}}\div\purpleE4=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$
Each person will get $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ of the bag of peanuts.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amos is painting his pots. He needs $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a liter to paint all four pots.  

**How much paint will he need for each of his $4$ pots if he uses the same amount of paint on each?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a liter
hints:
Amos has $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of a liter of paint. He wants to split the paint between $\blueE{4}$ pots.

So, $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\div\blueE4$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of a liter is $1$ liter divided into $4$ equal pieces.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\purpleE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$  piece into $\blueE4$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueE4$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{16}}$.
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\div\purpleE4=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{16}}$
Amos needs  $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{16}}$ of a liter of paint for each of his pots.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tommy will spend $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of his time this weekend studying for his $4$ final exams.  

**What fraction of the weekend will he spend studying for each of his $4$ exams if he spends the same amount of time studying for each exam?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of the weekend
hints:
Tommy has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$ of his weekend to spend on exams. He wants to split his time between $\blueD{4}$ exams.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}\div\blueD4$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$ of a weekend is $1$ weekend divided into $5$ equal pieces.  
 
[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$  piece into $\blueD4$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD4$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{5}}\div\greenD4=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$
Tommy will spend $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$ of the weekend studying for each of his final exams.

assessment_items.question_content:
Andy painted at his house. He used $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of his paint to paint $5$ walls.  

**What fraction of his paint did Andy use on each wall if he used the same amount of paint on each?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of his paint
hints:
Andy has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of his paint to use on his walls. He wants to split his paint between $\blueD{5}$ walls.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\div\blueD5$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of Andy's paint is all of his paint divided into $4$ equal pieces.  
 
[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$  piece into $\blueD5$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD5$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\div\greenD5=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$
Andy used  $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$ of his paint on each wall.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sara decides to bottle $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of her milk.  She pours the milk evenly among $5$ bottles. 

**What fraction of her milk will she put in each bottle$?$**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of her milk
hints:
Sara has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ of her milk to bottle. She wants to split this milk between $\blueD{5}$ bottles.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}\div\blueD5$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ of Sara's milk is all of her milk divided into $3$ equal pieces.  
 
[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$  piece into $\blueD5$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD5$.  


[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{15}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{3}}\div\greenD5=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{15}}$
Sara will put $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{15}}$ of her milk in each bottle.

assessment_items.question_content:
Paris has decided to give $\dfrac{1}{8}$ of her perfume to her $3$ closest friends. 

**What fraction of the perfume will she give to each of her $3$ friends if she gives each of them the same amount of perfume?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of her perfume
hints:
Paris has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ of her perfume to share. She wants to split this perfume between $\blueD{3}$ friends.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\div\blueD3$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ of Paris's perfume is all of her perfume divided into $8$ equal pieces.  
 
[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$  piece into $\blueD3$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD3$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{24}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\div\greenD3=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{24}}$
Paris will give each of her friends $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{24}}$ of her perfume.

assessment_items.question_content:
Susan has $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a bottle of water.  She wants to share it equally among herself and $2$  friends.  

**What fraction of the bottle of water would each person get?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a bottle
hints:
Susan has $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$ of her bottle of water to share. She wants to split her bottle between $\blueD{3}$ people.

So, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}\div\blueD3$ will give us our answer.

We know that $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$ of the bottle of water is $1$ bottle of water divided into $6$ equal pieces.  

[[☃ image 1]]

We can divide each $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$  piece into $\blueD3$ equal pieces to show division by $\blueD3$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Each of these new smaller pieces is equal $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}$.
$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{6}}\div\greenD3=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}$
Each person will get $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}$ of the bottle of water.

assessment_items.question_content:
Matt is filling containers of rice.  Each container holds $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a kilogram of rice.  

**If Matt has $7$ kilograms of rice, how many containers can he fill?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] containers
hints:
Matt has $\greenD7$ total kilograms of rice. Each container holds $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kg of rice.

So, $\greenD7\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\greenD7$ rectangles to represent the $\greenD7$ kilograms of rice.



[[☃ image 1]]

Since each container holds $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ of a kilogram, we  divide each of the $\greenD7$ kilograms of rice into $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kilogram portions.


[[☃ image 1]]





How many $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kilogram portions of rice are there?

There are $\blueD{28}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kilogram of rice.

$\greenD7\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}=\blueD{28}$
Matt can fill $\blueD{28}$ containers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Priscilla has some grapes that she wants to give to her friends.  She wants to give $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a kilogram of grapes to each friend.  

**If Priscilla has $6$ kilograms of grapes, to how many friends can she give grapes?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] friends
hints:
Priscilla has $\greenD6$ total kilograms of grapes. Each friend will get $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kg of grapes.

So, $\greenD6\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\greenD6$ rectangles to represent the $\greenD6$ kilograms of grapes.


[[☃ image 1]]

Since each friend will get $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ of a kilogram, we divide each of the $\greenD6$ kilograms of grapes into $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kilogram portions.

[[☃ image 1]]





How many $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kilogram portions of grapes are there?

There are $\blueD{24}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ kilogram of grapes.

$\greenD6\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}=\blueD{24}$
Priscilla can share with $\blueD{24}$ friends.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sally has been measuring a flower for a science project.  The flower has grown $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a centimeter each day and grown a total of $2$ centimeters taller.  

**How many days has Sally been measuring this flower?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] days
hints:
The flower has grown $\purpleE2$ total centimeters taller. Each day, the flower grew by $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ centimeters.

So, $\purpleE2\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\purpleE2$ rectangles to represent the $\purpleE2$ centimeters grown.

[[☃ image 1]]

Since each day the flower grew by $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ of a centimeter, we divide each of the $\purpleE2$ centimers grown into $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ centimeter portions.

[[☃ image 1]]

How many $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ centimeter portions are there?

There are $\blueE{4}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ centimeters of growth.

$\purpleE2\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}=\blueE{4}$
Sally has been measuring the flower for $\blueE{4}$ days.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bethany has some coffee that she wants to give to her friends.  She wants to give $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a liter of coffee to each friend.  

**If Bethany has $6$ liters of coffee to give to her friends, how many friends can she share her coffee with?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] friends
hints:
Bethany has $\purpleE6$ total liters of coffee. Each friend will get $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ of a liter of coffee.

So, $\purpleE6\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\purpleE6$ rectangles to represent the $\purpleE6$ liters of coffee.


[[☃ image 1]]

Since each friend will get $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ of a liter, we  divide each of the $\purpleE6$ liters of coffee into $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ liter portions.

[[☃ image 1]]

How many $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ liter portions of coffee are there?

There are $\blueE{12}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ of a liter of coffee.

$\purpleE6\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}=\blueE{12}$
Bethany can share her coffee with $\blueE{12}$ friends.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Amy has $5$ bunches of grapes, which she is serving as a snack to a group of children. Each serving is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a bunch.  

**How many children can get a serving of grapes as a snack?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] children
hints:
Mrs. Amy has $\purpleE5$ total bunches of grapes. Each child will get $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ of a bunch of grapes.

So, $\purpleE5\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\purpleE5$ rectangles to represent the $\purpleE5$ bunches of grapes.


[[☃ image 1]]

Since each child will get $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ of a bunch, we  divide each of the $\purpleE5$ bunches of grapes into $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ bunch portions.

[[☃ image 1]]

How many $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ bunch portions of grapes are there?

There are $\blueE{20}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ of a bunch of grapes.

$\purpleE5\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}=\blueE{20}$
Mrs. Amy can give grapes to $\blueE{20}$ children.

assessment_items.question_content:
Leah decided to paint some of the rooms at her $51$-room hotel. She needs $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of a can of paint per room.  

**If Leah had $6$ cans of paint, how many rooms can she paint?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] rooms
hints:
Leah has $\greenD6$ cans of paint. Each room will get $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$ of a can of paint.

So, $\greenD6\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\greenD6$ rectangles to represent the $\greenD6$ cans of paint.

[[☃ image 1]]

Since each room will get $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$ of a can, we  divide each of the $\greenD6$ cans of paint into $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$ can portions.

[[☃ image 1]]



How many $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$ can portions of paint are there?

There are $\blueD{30}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$ can of paint.

$\greenD6\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{5}}=\blueD{30}$
Leah can paint $\blueD{30}$ rooms.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jamie is painting her house.  Each room takes $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a liter of paint.  

**If Jamie has $3$ liters of paint, how many rooms can she paint?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] rooms
hints:
Jamie has $\greenD3$ liters of paint. Each room will get $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ of a liter of paint.

So, $\greenD3\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ will give us our answer.

Let's use these $\greenD3$ rectangles to represent the $\greenD3$ liters of paint.

[[☃ image 1]]

Since each room will get $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ of a liter, we  divide each of the $\greenD3$ liters of paint into $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ liter portions.


[[☃ image 1]]



How many $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ liter portions of paint are there?

There are $\blueD{6}$ portions of $\maroonD{\dfrac12}$ liter of paint.

$\greenD3\div\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}=\blueD{6}$
Jamie can paint $\blueD{6}$ rooms.
